title: Flutter book
subtitle: Pages attached to a spine strip so the book fans in both directions
excerpt: The flutter book attaches individual folded pages to a spine strip on alternating sides, producing a book that cascades open in one direction or the other. It is one of the simplest non-sewn structures and needs no tools beyond a bone folder and scissors.
sourceNotes: The flutter or flag-book structure is documented in Hedi Kyle and Ulla Warchol, The Art of the Fold (Laurence King, 2018, copyright). Documented here as synthesised practice from contemporary bookbinding craft. No pre-1928 primary source applies to this modern structure.

glossaryTerms.definition: A long folded strip of paper or card that runs the full height of the book and acts as the spine to which individual pages are attached.
The alternating attachment of pages to opposite sides of a spine strip, creating a fanning or cascading action when the book is opened.

content.text: A flutter book is one long folded strip acting as a spine, with individual pages glued to it on alternating sides. When you hold the cover and let the book fall open, the pages cascade like a fan. Hold it the other way and it fans in the opposite direction. The structure is a modern invention but draws on the same logic as the accordion concertina: repeated folded units creating a rhythmic opening action.
What you need

Spine strip: a long strip of cartridge paper, 90 gsm, folded accordion-style into equal sections. For a 12-page flutter book with A6 pages (105 x 148 mm), cut a strip 1,200 x 55 mm and fold into 12 sections of 100 mm each, bone-folding each crease sharply.
Pages: 12 sheets of cartridge paper, 90 gsm, each cut to 100 x 148 mm (portrait). Grain running parallel to the short side.
Covers: two pieces of card or heavy cartridge, 110 x 155 mm, for front and back.
Adhesive: regular PVA or a glue stick. The attachment tab only needs to hold a single sheet; heavy-duty acid-free PVA is not required.
Making the spine strip
Fold the long strip in half, then in half again, then accordion-fold the resulting sections to produce the required number of sections. Bone-fold every crease firmly. The
spine strip
should collapse flat and stand up without bowing.
Attaching the pages
Extend the accordion spine flat on the bench. Number the sections 1 to 12 (or however many you have) lightly in pencil on the top face.
On odd-numbered sections (1, 3, 5 ...) apply a thin strip of PVA across the top face at the left-hand edge, 5 mm wide.
Press page 1 onto section 1, aligning the page's right edge with the left edge of the section. Hold for 30 seconds.
Flip the spine strip over. Apply PVA to even-numbered sections (2, 4, 6 ...) in the same position. Attach pages to these sections in the same way.
This is the
flutter fold
: pages on alternating sides cascade when the book opens.
Once all pages are attached, glue the cover pieces to the first and last sections of the spine strip.
Finishing

Press the finished book under a flat weight for 15 minutes while the PVA sets. Test the fan by holding the book at one cover and letting the pages fall: they should cascade without any page sticking to its neighbour.
Care
Store flat. The spine strip is the weakest point: repeated sharp flexing at the folds will eventually crease through. Handle the book by the covers, not by fanning the pages forcefully.
Variations

Make the spine strip from book cloth for a more durable structure. Use translucent tracing paper as the pages for a ghostly layered effect. Or print or draw on each page before attaching, so the cascade reveals a sequential image.
